elements.sent-2411-001: One potential factor that could impede searches for the stochastic gravitational-wave background (SGWB), arising from the incoherent superposition of a multitude of weak and unresolvable gravitational-wave signals in the Universe, is correlated magnetic noise at Earth-scale distances, such as the Schumann resonances.
elements.sent-2411-002: As the sensitivity of terrestrial detectors to SGWBs increases, these effects are expected to become even more pronounced, emphasizing the importance of considering correlated noise sources in SGWB searches.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this study, we explore for the first time the impact of this class of noise on anisotropic SGWB searches.
elements.sent-2411-004: We focus on the low-frequency range of 20-200 Hz with a resolution of 1/32 Hz, using data from the LEMI-120 and Metronix MFS-06e magnetometers, located at the sites of the Advanced LIGO and Advanced Virgo interferometers, respectively, during the third observing run.
elements.sent-2411-005: These measurements were then compared with the directional upper limit derived from gravitational-wave data collected during the third observing run of the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA collaboration.
elements.sent-2411-006: The results indicate that the magnetic noise correlations are below the interferometers' sensitivity level during the O3 observing run.
elements.sent-2411-007: The potential impact of magnetic correlations on the Advanced LIGO detectors has also been investigated at the A+ design sensitivity level.
elements.sent-2411-008: As of today, the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA (LVK) collaboration \cite{KAGRA:2013rdx,aLIGO:2020wna,Tse:2019wcy,LIGOScientific:2014pky,Virgo:2019juy,VIRGO:2014yos,Aso:2013eba,KAGRA:2020tym has cataloged 90 compact binary coalescences (CBCs) gravitational-wave (GW) events \cite{LIGOScientific:2018mvr,LIGOScientific:2020ibl,KAGRA:2021vkt from the first three observing runs O1, O2, and O3.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: With the ongoing fourth observing run (O4), one detection of CBC event is being reported every few days.
elements.sent-2411-010: This observing run may lead to the first detection of other kinds of GW signals, such as quasi-monochromatic GWs, short-duration unmodeled GW transients, as well as persistent and unmodeled GWs, namely stochastic gravitational wave backgrounds (SGWBs).
elements.sent-2411-011: An SGWB results from the superposition of individually undetectable GW signals, and its detection would be an observational milestone of GW astronomy with ground-based GW interferometers.
elements.sent-2411-012: An SGWB signal can originate from either the primordial Universe or astrophysical sources.
elements.sent-2411-013: An SGWB from cosmological phenomena \cite{Caprini:2018mtu , such as the inflationary era \cite{Turner:1996ck,Easther:2006gt of the primordial Universe, first-order phase transitions \cite{Marzola:2017jzl , cosmic strings \cite{Kibble:1976sj,Sarangi:2002yt,Damour:2004kw,Siemens:2006yp , domain walls \cite{Martin:1996ea , and primordial black holes \cite{Mandic:2016lcn could provide unique insights into the evolution of the Universe, that are inaccessible by any other means.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: This kind of SGWB is expected to be predominantly isotropic, similar to the Cosmic Microwave Background \cite{1965ApJ...142..419P .}
elements.sent-2411-015: An astrophysical SGWB from isolated neutron stars \cite{Rosado:2012bk , compact binary coalescences \cite{LIGOScientific:2021qlt,Zhu:2011bd , core-collapse supernovae \cite{Buonanno:2004tp , and stellar core collapses \cite{Crocker:2015taa could provide new insights in astrophysical populations and evolution processes.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: In contrast to a cosmological SGWB, an astrophysical SGWB in the nearby Universe follows the distribution of the matter in space, such as galaxies or galaxy clusters, and is likely to show a degree of spatial anisotropy.
elements.sent-2411-017: To target SGWB anisotropies from localized sources with a network of ground-based GW detectors, one cross-correlates the data streams from different detector pairs \cite{Allen:1997ad,Romano:2016dpx .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The principle of radiometry, which takes advantage of the Earth's rotation \cite{Ballmer:2005uw,Mitra:2007mc , is then employed to produce sky-maps of the GW power in the sky \cite{KAGRA:2021mth,KAGRA:2021rmt .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Radiometer searches for SGWB anisotropies can either target narrow-band sources in a specific sky direction or broad-band sources emitting GW over a wide frequency band and are known in the literature as narrow-band radiometer and broad-band radiometer (BBR) searches \cite{KAGRA:2021mth .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Recently, an all-sky, all-frequency (ASAF) radiometer analysis was made available to produce sky maps of GW power at any frequency \cite{KAGRA:2021rmt .}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, none of these searches has found any SGWB signal in ground-based GW detectors' data until today, hence setting upper limits on the SGWB intensity \cite{KAGRA:2021mth,KAGRA:2021rmt .}
elements.sent-2411-022: These analyses assume that there is no correlated noise source between the detectors.
elements.sent-2411-023: However, multiple studies have shown that persistent global magnetic interferences \cite{Thrane:2013npa,Janssens:2022tdj can couple to the GW interferometers, giving rise to a correlated noise among the detectors of the network.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Even though no impact is currently observed, projections predict significant coupling for Virgo and LIGO at A+ design sensitivity \cite{A+,Janssens:2022tdj , as well as for future detectors such as the Einstein Telescope \cite{Janssens:2021cta .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Such correlated noise in GW detectors is a non-negligible obstacle for detecting and characterizing the SGWB.
elements.sent-2411-026: A significant source of correlated magnetic noise is the Schumann resonances \cite{Schumann+1952+149+154, Schumann+1952+250+252, Jackson:1998nia .}
elements.sent-2411-027: These resonances are electromagnetic excitations inside the conducting cavity between the Earth and the ionosphere, acting as a spherical waveguide.
elements.sent-2411-028: Atmospheric discharges, such as cloud-to-ground lightning strikes worldwide, generate them.
elements.sent-2411-029: The spectrum of the Schumann resonances presents a series of broad spectral peaks ranging in the 8-60 Hz frequency band, whose amplitude varies depending on the season and location \cite{Meyers:2020qrb .}
elements.sent-2411-030: In addition to Schumann resonances, correlations due to magnetic fields can also occur at higher frequencies, originating from the superposition of individual lightning strikes propagating over long distances on Earth before degrading \cite{Janssens:2022tdj .}
elements.sent-2411-031: In the near future, as the GW detectors approach their design sensitivities, correlated magnetic noise may no longer be negligible when performing SGWB searches.
elements.sent-2411-032: Many Wiener filtering-based studies have been developed to subtract this noise \cite{Thrane:2014yza,Coughlin:2016vor,Coughlin:2018tjc .}
elements.sent-2411-033: In addition to that, the analytical modeling of the correlated noise to assess the impact on the detectors has been extensively studied \cite{Himemoto:2017gnw, Himemoto:2019iwd .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Alternatively, Fisher-matrix approaches are available in the literature, such as in \citet{Himemoto:2023keu .}
elements.sent-2411-035: In this paper, we focus for the first time on the impact of correlated magnetic noise on searches for anisotropic SGWB with Earth-based detectors.
elements.sent-2411-036: To assess the potential contamination of these searches by correlated magnetic noise, we separately measure this noise.
elements.sent-2411-037: Then, we compare this term with the ASAF strain upper limits, as well as the BBR upper limits of GW flux obtained from the O3 run of the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA collaboration \cite{KAGRA:2021rmt,KAGRA:2021mth .}
elements.sent-2411-038: We also perform a comparison between these measurements and the sensitivity of the ASAF directional search, once the LIGO detectors reach the A+ design configuration \cite{A+,Cooper:2022jfr .}
elements.sent-2411-039: The structure of the paper is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-040: In Section \ref{sec:CC_with_correlated_noise , we outline the cross-correlation statistic in the presence of correlated magnetic noise.}
elements.sent-2411-041: In Section \ref{sec:magnetic_noise_measurement , we discuss the instruments used to measure the noise, the pre-processing of data applied in this analysis and the coupling mechanism of magnetic correlations with the GW interferometers.}
elements.sent-2411-042: In Section \ref{sec:directional_searches_with_correlated_magnetic_noise , we develop the formalism to evaluate the impact of correlated magnetic noise on directional SGWB searches .}
elements.sent-2411-043: In Section \ref{sec:results , we present the main results of this work, namely the estimates of the effective SGWB spectra that arise from the magnetic noise in the current ground-based GW detector network for different SGWB directional searches, which we call magnetic budgets.}
elements.sent-2411-045: In appendix \ref{sec:appendix_ASAF_HL_HV_LV , we present the GW strain for each sky direction, induced by correlated magnetic terms for each baseline individually of the LVK detector network.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Lastly, in appendix \ref{sec:appendix_A+_projection , we perform a projection of an power-law integrated-like (PI-like) sensitivity curve at each sky direction for the ASAF directional search, to explore the impact of magnetic correlations on LIGO detectors once they reach the A+ design sensitivity levels, followed by the derivation of the method in appendix \ref{sec:appendix_PI_curve .}}
elements.section-2411-002: Cross-Correlation statistic IN THE PRESENCE OF CORRELATED MAGNETIC NOISE
elements.sent-2411-047: Anisotropic SGWB searches aim at characterizing the angular distribution of the dimensionless GW energy density parameter $\Omega_{\mathrm{gw (f,\boldsymbol{\hat{n )$ \cite{KAGRA:2021rmt,LIGOScientific:2019gaw,KAGRA:2021mth,Romano:2016dpx,Thrane:2009fp , \begin{equation} \label{Omega_mag} \Omega_{\mathrm{gw}}(f,\boldsymbol{\hat{n}}) =\frac{1}{\rho_c}\frac{\mathrm{d}^3\rho_{\rm gw}}{\mathrm{d}({\mathrm{ln}f}) \mathrm{d}^{2}{\boldsymbol{\hat{n}}}}=\frac{2 \pi^2}{3H_0^2}f^3 \mathcal{P}_{\rm gw}(f,\boldsymbol{\hat{n}}),\end{equation} where $d\rho_{\rm gw}$ denotes the GW energy density per logarithmic frequency unit $\mathrm{d (\mathrm{ln f)$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The quantity $\rho_c$ expresses the critical energy density for a spatially flat Universe, with $H_0 = 67.9 \rm km s^{-1 Mpc^{-1}$ being the Hubble parameter today according to the Planck 2015 measurements \cite{Planck:2015fie and $\mathcal{P _{\rm gw (f,\boldsymbol{\hat{n )$ characterizes the angular power spectrum of the SGWB.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Under the assumption of a Gaussian, unpolarized and stationary GWB, the one-sided $\mathcal{P _{\rm gw (f,\boldsymbol{\hat{n )$ is related to the expectation value of the GW strain distribution $h_A(f,\boldsymbol{\hat{n )$ across different sky directions as \cite{Romano:2016dpx \begin{equation} \langle h_A^*(f, \boldsymbol{\hat{n}}) h_{A'}(f', \boldsymbol{\hat{n}}') \rangle = \frac{1}{4} \mathcal{P}_{\rm gw}(f, \boldsymbol{\hat{n}}) \delta_{AA'} \delta(f - f') \delta(\boldsymbol{\hat{n}}, \boldsymbol{\hat{n}}'),\end{equation} where the index A expresses the GW polarization index ($\{\rm +, \rm x\}$), and the asterisk denotes the complex conjugate of the GW strain amplitude.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: The SGWB signal is expected to be both weak and inherently random.
elements.sent-2411-051: As a result, distinguishing this type of signal from instrumental noise on a single detector poses a significant challenge.
elements.sent-2411-052: To address this, the statistical method commonly used in SGWB searches is the cross-correlation \cite{Allen:1997ad,Romano:2016dpx of data streams from a detector pair.}
elements.sent-2411-053: However, the detectors' output can also contain contributions from correlated noise.
elements.sent-2411-054: In this work, we will consider correlated noise from magnetic origin, which exhibits correlations over Earth-scale distances \cite{Janssens:2022tdj .}

elements.sent-2411-059: After cross-correlating $\tilde{s _{I (f)$ and $\tilde{s _{J (f)$, the expectation value is \begin{equation}\label{expected_value} \begin{split} \langle \tilde{s}^{*}_{I}(f) \tilde{s}^{}_{J}(f') \rangle = \langle \tilde{h}^{*}_{I}(f) \tilde{h}^{}_{J}(f') \rangle + \cancel{\langle \tilde{n}^{* \mathrm{indep}}_{I}(f) \tilde{n}^{{\mathrm{indep}}}_{J}(f') \rangle} + \langle \tilde{n}^{* \mathrm{mag}}_{I}(f) \tilde{n}^{{\mathrm{mag}}}_{J}(f') \rangle , \end{split}\end{equation} where the term $\langle \tilde{n ^{* \mathrm{indep _{I (f) \tilde{n ^{{\mathrm{indep _{J (f') \rangle$ vanishes, because there is no correlation of instrumental noise, due to the geographical separation of detectors.}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-070: Magnetic noise can couple to the GW interferometers, resulting in an additional source of noise for the GW strain in the data.
elements.sent-2411-071: To correctly measure the extra noise strain spectral density from correlated magnetic noise in SGWB searches, it is important to understand how this coupling mechanism works at different GW detector sites.
elements.sent-2411-072: The coupling mechanism varies for different frequency ranges.
elements.sent-2411-073: Specifically, in the 20-100 Hz band, global magnetic fields, mainly Schumann resonances, induce forces on the magnets located on the electromagnetic actuators of the test masses and on suspension systems as well \cite{Thrane:2013npa,LSC:2018vzm .}
elements.sent-2411-074: Other coupling mechanisms may be possible at higher frequencies, such as the interaction with the cabling system \cite{Janssens:2022tdj .}
elements.sent-2411-075: To quantify the coupling of the magnetic field to the GW-strain channels, one has to measure the magnetic coupling function $\kappa_{I (f)$ for the detector $I$ \cite{Janssens:2022tdj (the same applies for the detector $J$), which is considered as the product of two time-independent quantities\footnote{In reality the coupling function changes to some extent over long observation periods.}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: However, as shown in \cite{Janssens:2022tdj , any uncertainty related to time fluctuations is second order, and hence, we can ignore any time dependence in this work. \begin{equation} \label{coupling_total} \kappa_{I}(f)= \kappa_{{I}, \mathrm{OTI}}(f) \cdot \kappa_{{I}, \mathrm{ITC}}(f),\end{equation} where $\kappa_{{I , \mathrm{ITC (f)$ is called inside-to-GW-channel (ITC) coupling function and $\kappa_{{I , \mathrm{OTI (f)$ outside-to-inside (OTI) coupling function.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The key coupling features are captured in the ITC coupling function, while the OTI coupling provides a level of shielding/amplification caused by the building.

elements.section-2411-004: Directional searches and correlated magnetic noise
elements.sent-2411-086: When searching for the anisotropies of SGWB in particular directions in the sky with ground-based GW detectors \cite{KAGRA:2021rmt,LIGOScientific:2019gaw,KAGRA:2021mth , one employs cross-correlation techniques with the inclusion of a time-varying phase delay.}
elements.sent-2411-087: This delay expresses the required time difference of a GW signal to arrive at the detectors coming from a specific sky direction.
elements.sent-2411-088: Thus, by cross-correlating the data, GW signals from specific directions in the sky will interfere constructively.

elements.sent-2411-091: Following the prescription of the radiometer analysis \cite{Ballmer:2005uw,Mitra:2007mc , one can derive the maximum-likelihood estimator for $\mathcal{P _{\rm gw (f,\boldsymbol{\hat{n )$ as \begin{equation} \label{estimator} \boldsymbol{\hat{\mathcal{P}}}_{\rm gw}(f) = \boldsymbol{\Gamma}^{-1}_{\rm gw}(f) \vdot \boldsymbol{X}_{\rm gw}(f),\end{equation} where $\boldsymbol{X _{\rm gw}$ and $\boldsymbol{\Gamma _{\rm gw}$ are called dirty map and Fisher matrix and are defined as \begin{equation} \label{Dirty} X^{IJ}_{{\rm gw}_p}(f)= 2 \Delta f \mathfrak{Re} \sum_{t} \frac{\gamma^{IJ*}_{ft,p}\tilde{s}^{*}_{I}(t;f)\tilde{s}_{J}(t;f)}{P_{{I}}(t;f)P_{{J}}(t;f)},\end{equation} \begin{equation} \label{Fisher} \Gamma^{IJ}_{{\rm gw}_{pp'}}(f) = \frac{\tau \Delta f}{2} \mathfrak{Re} \sum_{t} \frac{\gamma^{IJ*}_{ft,p} \gamma^{IJ}_{ft,p'}}{P_{{I}}(t;f)P_{{J}}(t;f)},\end{equation} with $\tau$ the segment duration, $\Delta f$ the frequency resolution, and $P_I(t;f)$, $P_J(t;f)$ the power spectral densities of the detectors $I$ and $J$.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-110: These signals cannot be detected either by broad-band or narrow-band radiometer searches, as broad-band radiometry focuses on sources that emit GWs over a wide frequency band, while narrow-band radiometry historically targets only known sky locations.

elements.sent-2411-112: This indicates the suitability of the search for quasi-monochromatic GW signals from sources such as isolated, rotating, non-axisymmetric neutron stars.

elements.section-2411-009: Derivation of the PI-like sensitivity curves for SGWB radiometer searches in the absence of pixel-to-pixel correlations
elements.sent-2411-192: In this appendix, we sketch the derivation of the formula we have employed to produce the projections of the sensitivity of A+ LIGO detectors \cite{A+ for SGWB radiometer searches.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Given the absence of such sensitivity curves in the literature, we derive them here for the first time, taking inspiration from the power-law integrated (PI) sensitivity curves that exist for searches for isotropic SGWB \cite{Thrane:2013oya .}
elements.sent-2411-194: Throughout the derivation, we make three assumptions, namely that 1) we are performing a radiometer analysis, 2) we neglect pixel-to-pixel correlations, and 3) we can approximate detectors' noise power spectral densities to be constant over time.
elements.sent-2411-195: As a consequence, the expectation value of the cross-correlation statistic ${\rm CSD (t;\,f) \equiv \tilde{s ^{* _{I (t;f)\tilde{s _{J (t;f)$ becomes \begin{equation}\label{eq:CC_radiometry_expectation_value_for_PI} \expval{{\rm CSD}_{IJ} (t; \, f)} = \frac{\tau}{2}\int_{\mathcal{S}_{2}} \dd[2]{\vu*{n}} \gamma_{IJ}(t; \, f, \, \vu*{n})\, \mathcal{P_{\rm gw}}(f, \, \vu*{n}) = \frac{\tau}{2} \gamma_{IJ}(t; \, f,\, \vu*{n}_{0})\, \mathcal{P}_{\vu*{n}_{0}}(f),\end{equation} where one has used Eq. \eqref{eq:P_f_n_radiometer_for_PI in the second equality, with $\tau$ and the geometrical factor $\gamma_{IJ (t; \, f\, \vu*{n )$ having the same meaning as in the main text.}}}}}}}}}
